questions.q: A High Court is the highest court of justice at which level?
questions.explanation: A High Court is the highest court within a state or group of states.

questions.q: Which is the oldest High Court in India?
questions.explanation: The Calcutta High Court, established in 1862, is the oldest High Court in India.

questions.q: In which year was the Calcutta High Court established?
questions.explanation: The Calcutta High Court was established in 1862, making it the oldest in India.

questions.q: Who is the head of a High Court?
options: The Chief Justice of the High Court
questions.explanation: Each High Court is headed by its Chief Justice.

questions.q: Who appoints the judges of a High Court?
questions.explanation: Judges of a High Court are appointed by the President of India.

questions.q: High Courts exercise supervisory jurisdiction over which courts?
questions.explanation: High Courts exercise jurisdiction and superintendence over subordinate courts within their territory.

questions.q: Up to what age may a judge of a High Court hold office?
questions.explanation: A High Court judge holds office until the age of 62 years.

questions.q: A single High Court can have jurisdiction over how many states or union territories?
options: One or more states and union territories
questions.explanation: A common High Court may exercise jurisdiction over more than one state or union territory.

questions.q: Which level of judiciary lies directly above the High Courts?
questions.explanation: Appeals from the High Courts lie to the Supreme Court, which is above them.

questions.q: Along with the Calcutta High Court, two other High Courts were established in 1862 at which cities?
questions.explanation: The Bombay and Madras High Courts were also established in 1862, alongside Calcutta.

questions.q: High Courts are dealt with under which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The High Courts for States are dealt with under Part VI of the Constitution.

questions.q: Which article provides for a High Court for each State?
questions.explanation: Article 214 provides that there shall be a High Court for each State.

questions.q: Under which article can a High Court issue writs, including for purposes other than enforcement of Fundamental Rights?
questions.explanation: Article 226 empowers High Courts to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights and for any other purpose.

questions.q: The writ jurisdiction of a High Court under Article 226 is considered wider than that of the Supreme Court under Article 32 in which respect?
options: It can be used for any legal right, not only Fundamental Rights
questions.explanation: Article 226 allows writs for any legal right, while Article 32 is confined to Fundamental Rights, making the High Court's writ power broader in scope.

questions.q: The power of superintendence of a High Court over all courts and tribunals in its territory is provided by which article?
questions.explanation: Article 227 gives every High Court power of superintendence over all courts and tribunals within its jurisdiction.

questions.q: A judge of a High Court can be removed from office through which process?
options: By the same special-majority parliamentary process as a Supreme Court judge
questions.explanation: A High Court judge is removed by the President after a special-majority address by both Houses of Parliament, like a Supreme Court judge.

questions.q: The President can transfer a judge from one High Court to another after consultation with which authority?
questions.explanation: Under Article 222, the President may transfer a judge after consultation with the Chief Justice of India.

questions.q: Who administers the oath of office to a judge of a High Court?
options: The Governor of the State or someone appointed by them
questions.explanation: A High Court judge takes the oath before the Governor of the State or a person appointed by the Governor.

questions.q: To qualify for appointment as a High Court judge, a person must, among other routes, have held judicial office or practised as an advocate for at least how many years?
questions.explanation: A qualifying requirement is at least ten years of judicial office or practice as an advocate of a High Court.

questions.q: The Constitution permits Parliament to establish a common High Court for which combination?
options: Two or more States or union territories
A State and a foreign court
questions.explanation: Article 231 allows Parliament to establish a common High Court for two or more States or for States and union territories.

questions.q: Which High Court, established in 1866, has its principal seat at Prayagraj and is among the oldest in India?
questions.explanation: The Allahabad High Court, established in 1866, has its principal seat at Prayagraj (Allahabad).

questions.q: A retired High Court judge is generally permitted to plead before which courts?
options: The Supreme Court and other High Courts, but not the High Court where they served
Only the High Court where they served
questions.explanation: A retired High Court judge may practise in the Supreme Court and other High Courts but not in the High Court where they served.

questions.q: Who was the first woman judge of a High Court in India, appointed to the Kerala High Court?
questions.explanation: Anna Chandy became the first woman High Court judge in India when appointed to the Kerala High Court in 1959.

questions.q: Who was the first woman to become Chief Justice of a High Court in India?
questions.explanation: Leila Seth became the first woman Chief Justice of a High Court when she headed the Himachal Pradesh High Court in 1991.

questions.q: Where a vacancy occurs in the office of Chief Justice of a High Court, who appoints an acting Chief Justice?
questions.explanation: Under Article 223, the President appoints an acting Chief Justice of a High Court when the office is vacant.

questions.q: Additional and acting judges of a High Court may be appointed under which article?
questions.explanation: Article 224 provides for the appointment of additional and acting judges of a High Court.

questions.q: The salaries and allowances of High Court judges are charged on which fund?
options: The Consolidated Fund of the State
questions.explanation: The salaries of High Court judges are charged on the Consolidated Fund of the State, while their pensions are charged on the Consolidated Fund of India.

questions.q: Several northeastern states share a common High Court that has its principal seat at which city?
questions.explanation: The Gauhati High Court, with its principal seat at Guwahati, has historically served several northeastern states.

questions.q: The collegium that recommends High Court appointments to the President is headed by which authority at the national stage of consultation?
questions.explanation: High Court appointments involve a collegium process in which the Chief Justice of India plays the leading role in consultation.

questions.q: A High Court is a court of record under which article, giving it power to punish for its own contempt?
questions.explanation: Article 215 declares every High Court to be a court of record with power to punish for contempt of itself.
